system_prompt:
You are a senior dermoscopy expert and knowledge engineer reviewing a proposed
classification rule before it is tested on images.

You will be given:
1. A candidate rule and its pre-conditions
2. The pair of classes it is meant to distinguish (favored class vs other class)

Your task: evaluate whether each pre-condition is a reliable dermoscopic discriminator.

For each pre-condition, rate it as one of:
- "reliable"          — feature consistently separates the favored class from the other;
                        rarely or never present in the other class
- "unreliable"        — feature can easily occur in both classes, is too vague,
                        or points in the wrong direction
- "context_dependent" — only discriminating under specific co-occurring conditions;
                        risky as a stand-alone gate

Then give an overall recommendation:
- "accept"  — all or most pre-conditions are reliable; safe to proceed to image validation
- "revise"  — one or more pre-conditions are unreliable; flag them before image validation
- "reject"  — the rule's core logic is fundamentally flawed; do not spend image-validation
               budget on it

Output ONLY a JSON object (no markdown, no commentary):
{
  "precondition_ratings": [
    {
      "precondition": "<exact text of pre-condition>",
      "rating": "reliable|unreliable|context_dependent",
      "comment": "<one-sentence clinical justification>"
    }
  ],
  "overall": "accept|revise|reject",
  "rationale": "<two-to-three sentence overall assessment>"
}
text_content:
Favored class: Melanoma
Other class: Melanocytic Nevus

Rule:
When a pigmented lesion is asymmetric AND shows three or more distinct colors (e.g. dark brown plus gray-blue plus pink/red or white) AND has an irregular/notched border, classify as Melanoma. Do NOT require ulceration — these dermoscopic features alone are sufficient evidence of melanoma; nevi are typically symmetric, one-or-two-toned brown, with smooth borders.

Pre-conditions:
  1. Lesion is clearly asymmetric — one half is not a mirror image of the other in shape OR pigment distribution
  2. At least three distinct colors are visible within the lesion (e.g. light brown, dark brown/black, gray-blue, pink-red, white regression area)
  3. Border is irregular, notched, or scalloped — not smooth/round/oval
  4. Pigment distribution is heterogeneous (dark patches next to lighter or pink/white areas), not a uniform brown of a banal nevus
  5. DO NOT demand ulceration, bleeding, or clinical history of growth — those are NOT required for a dermoscopic melanoma call; the visual ABCD-style features above are themselves sufficient
  6. An atypical pigment network (broadened, branched, abruptly cut off at the periphery) OR streaks/pseudopods radiating from the edge OR a blue-white veil OR irregular dots/globules unevenly distributed is present — at least one of these melanoma-specific dermoscopic structures
  7. The lesion is NOT a uniform reticular network with regular meshwork and gradual fade-out at the periphery (the classic banal nevus pattern)
  8. The lesion does NOT consist of evenly-sized, evenly-distributed brown globules in a symmetric arrangement (the classic globular nevus pattern)

Please evaluate each pre-condition and provide an overall recommendation.
